lessons.title: Hematology: Practical MLT Exam Prep (Unit 1)
lessons.summary: Career-specific MLT lesson for ASCP-MLT-style exam preparation. Domain focus: Hematology. Practical workflows, safety, documentation, and scope-appropriate judgment—exam prep only, not workplace protocols for every jurisdiction.
learningObjectives: Explain core concepts for Hematology as tested on ASCP-MLT and in typical MLT practice settings.
Apply safety, quality, and communication steps appropriate to MLT scope.
Recognize common exam distractors that confuse procedure with policy or exceed scope.
Use a structured approach to documentation and escalation when findings are outside normal parameters.

sections.heading: Domain overview and exam relevance
sections.body: Hematology: Practical MLT Exam Prep (Unit 1) maps to Hematology within MLT. Allied exams reward repeatable safety habits: verify orders, verify patient identity, use checklists for high-risk steps, and know what must be escalated to a supervisor or licensed clinician. Keep content practical: what you would do on shift, what you would chart, and what you would refuse if it exceeds scope.

sections.heading: Procedures, equipment, and quality
sections.body: Study the sequence: preparation → execution → monitoring → cleanup/handoff. For lab and imaging tracks, emphasize pre-analytical errors, QC concepts, ALARA, and contrast precautions as applicable. For therapy assistants, emphasize guarding, line/tube awareness, vitals before activity, and reporting pain or new neuro signs. For pharmacy tech, emphasize calculations verification, look-alike/sound-alike risk, and sterile compounding discipline when tested.

sections.heading: Patient interaction and interprofessional practice
sections.body: Communication items often test therapeutic professionalism, privacy, consent, and clear handoffs. Choose answers that respect autonomy, reduce harm, and maintain chain of accountability. When a stem implies an emergency, prioritize life-safety actions within your certification scope and rapid activation of appropriate resources.

sections.heading: Exam traps and decision rules
sections.body: Eliminate options that skip verification, invent independent clinical decisions outside scope, ignore infection control, or document before doing the task. When two answers sound correct, pick the one that matches the most immediate safety requirement and the clearest policy-aligned action for your role.

examTips: Tag this card: ASCP-MLT / MLT.
Scope questions: if an action requires independent clinical judgment beyond your role, it is usually wrong for assistant/tech items.
Safety beats speed on practical exams—choose the option that prevents harm first.
Read units and settings carefully; calculation items punish decimal placement errors.

lessons.title: Radiographic Positioning: Practical Imaging Exam Prep (Unit 1)
lessons.summary: Career-specific Imaging lesson for ARRT-RAD-style exam preparation. Domain focus: Radiographic Positioning. Practical workflows, safety, documentation, and scope-appropriate judgment—exam prep only, not workplace protocols for every jurisdiction.
learningObjectives: Explain core concepts for Radiographic Positioning as tested on ARRT-RAD and in typical Imaging practice settings.
Apply safety, quality, and communication steps appropriate to Imaging scope.
Recognize common exam distractors that confuse procedure with policy or exceed scope.
Use a structured approach to documentation and escalation when findings are outside normal parameters.

sections.heading: Domain overview and exam relevance
sections.body: Radiographic Positioning: Practical Imaging Exam Prep (Unit 1) maps to Radiographic Positioning within Imaging. Allied exams reward repeatable safety habits: verify orders, verify patient identity, use checklists for high-risk steps, and know what must be escalated to a supervisor or licensed clinician. Keep content practical: what you would do on shift, what you would chart, and what you would refuse if it exceeds scope.

sections.heading: Procedures, equipment, and quality
sections.body: Study the sequence: preparation → execution → monitoring → cleanup/handoff. For lab and imaging tracks, emphasize pre-analytical errors, QC concepts, ALARA, and contrast precautions as applicable. For therapy assistants, emphasize guarding, line/tube awareness, vitals before activity, and reporting pain or new neuro signs. For pharmacy tech, emphasize calculations verification, look-alike/sound-alike risk, and sterile compounding discipline when tested.

sections.heading: Patient interaction and interprofessional practice
sections.body: Communication items often test therapeutic professionalism, privacy, consent, and clear handoffs. Choose answers that respect autonomy, reduce harm, and maintain chain of accountability. When a stem implies an emergency, prioritize life-safety actions within your certification scope and rapid activation of appropriate resources.

sections.heading: Exam traps and decision rules
sections.body: Eliminate options that skip verification, invent independent clinical decisions outside scope, ignore infection control, or document before doing the task. When two answers sound correct, pick the one that matches the most immediate safety requirement and the clearest policy-aligned action for your role.

examTips: Tag this card: ARRT-RAD / Imaging.
Scope questions: if an action requires independent clinical judgment beyond your role, it is usually wrong for assistant/tech items.
Safety beats speed on practical exams—choose the option that prevents harm first.
Read units and settings carefully; calculation items punish decimal placement errors.

For lab and imaging tracks, emphasize pre-analytical errors, QC concepts, ALARA, and contrast precautions as applicable. For therapy assistants, emphasize guarding, line/tube awareness, vitals before activity, and reporting pain or new neuro signs. For pharmacy tech, emphasize calculations verification, look-alike/sound-alike risk, and sterile compounding discipline when tested.

sections.heading: Patient interaction and interprofessional practice
sections.body: Communication items often test therapeutic professionalism, privacy, consent, and clear handoffs. Choose answers that respect autonomy, reduce harm, and maintain chain of accountability. When a stem implies an emergency, prioritize life-safety actions within your certification scope and rapid activation of appropriate resources.

sections.heading: Exam traps and decision rules
sections.body: Eliminate options that skip verification, invent independent clinical decisions outside scope, ignore infection control, or document before doing the task. When two answers sound correct, pick the one that matches the most immediate safety requirement and the clearest policy-aligned action for your role.

examTips: Tag this card: PTCB / Pharmacy Technician.
Scope questions: if an action requires independent clinical judgment beyond your role, it is usually wrong for assistant/tech items.
Safety beats speed on practical exams—choose the option that prevents harm first.
Read units and settings carefully; calculation items punish decimal placement errors.

questions.stem: (ASCP-MLT; MLT; mlt-hematology-unit-01-01; Q1) Scenario: You are working as a MLT learner in a hematology context. Which action best demonstrates safe, scope-appropriate, exam-style practice?
choices: A. Follow policy, verify key identifiers and orders, perform the assigned task, and document objective findings
B. Skip hand hygiene to save time when gloves are available
C. Perform a delegated task without reporting an unexpected critical finding because “someone else will notice”
D. Accept a verbal order for a new medication from a family member and implement it immediately
questions.rationale: Correct practice centers on verification, scope, infection prevention, and timely reporting. MLT exams consistently punish shortcuts that bypass safety or chain of command. This item is exam preparation for Hematology and does not replace employer policy.

incorrectRationales.B: Hand hygiene and PPE rules are not optional shortcuts; gloves do not replace hand hygiene when indicated by policy.
incorrectRationales.C: Unexpected critical findings must be reported to the responsible licensed clinician or supervisor per protocol.
incorrectRationales.D: Technicians and assistants do not take medication orders from family members; orders must come through authorized channels.

questions.stem: (ASCP-MLT; MLT; mlt-hematology-unit-01-01; Q2) Scenario: You are working as a MLT learner in a hematology context. Which action best demonstrates safe, scope-appropriate, exam-style practice?
choices: A. Follow policy, verify key identifiers and orders, perform the assigned task, and document objective findings
B. Skip hand hygiene to save time when gloves are available
C. Perform a delegated task without reporting an unexpected critical finding because “someone else will notice”
D. Accept a verbal order for a new medication from a family member and implement it immediately
questions.rationale: Correct practice centers on verification, scope, infection prevention, and timely reporting. MLT exams consistently punish shortcuts that bypass safety or chain of command. This item is exam preparation for Hematology and does not replace employer policy.

incorrectRationales.C: Unexpected critical findings must be reported to the responsible licensed clinician or supervisor per protocol.
incorrectRationales.D: Technicians and assistants do not take medication orders from family members; orders must come through authorized channels.

questions.stem: (ASCP-MLT; MLT; mlt-urinalysis-unit-01-02; Q4) Scenario: You are working as a MLT learner in a urinalysis context. Which action best demonstrates safe, scope-appropriate, exam-style practice?
choices: A. Follow policy, verify key identifiers and orders, perform the assigned task, and document objective findings

questions.rationale: Correct practice centers on verification, scope, infection prevention, and timely reporting. MLT exams consistently punish shortcuts that bypass safety or chain of command. This item is exam preparation for Urinalysis and does not replace employer policy.

incorrectRationales.D: Technicians and assistants do not take medication orders from family members; orders must come through authorized channels.

questions.stem: (ASCP-MLT; MLT; mlt-urinalysis-unit-01-02; Q5) Select all that apply: Which actions reflect safe MLT practice in Urinalysis?
choices: A. Use two patient identifiers when performing tasks that could cause harm if misidentified
B. Report equipment malfunctions, near misses, or broken sterility barriers according to policy
C. Document care promptly with objective, concise language
D. Share patient information in a public elevator to save time
E. Ask for clarification when an instruction is unclear or appears outside training
questions.rationale: A, B, C, and E are standard safety and professionalism expectations across allied roles. D violates privacy and is never appropriate. This SATA reinforces practical exam habits for Urinalysis.

incorrectRationales.D: Protected health information must not be discussed in public spaces; privacy rules are strict on certification exams.

questions.stem: (PTCB; Pharmacy Technician; pharmacy-tech-dosage-calculations-unit-01-07; Q277) Scenario: You are working as a Pharmacy Technician learner in a dosage calculations context. Which action best demonstrates safe, scope-appropriate, exam-style practice?
choices: A. Follow policy, verify key identifiers and orders, perform the assigned task, and document objective findings

questions.stem: (PTCB; Pharmacy Technician; pharmacy-tech-drug-classifications-unit-01-08; Q280) Select all that apply: Which actions reflect safe Pharmacy Technician practice in Drug Classifications?
choices: A. Use two patient identifiers when performing tasks that could cause harm if misidentified
B. Report equipment malfunctions, near misses, or broken sterility barriers according to policy
C. Document care promptly with objective, concise language
D. Share patient information in a public elevator to save time
E. Ask for clarification when an instruction is unclear or appears outside training
questions.rationale: A, B, C, and E are standard safety and professionalism expectations across allied roles. D violates privacy and is never appropriate. This SATA reinforces practical exam habits for Drug Classifications.

incorrectRationales.D: Protected health information must not be discussed in public spaces; privacy rules are strict on certification exams.
